text: Hello, I'm sharing a robotic right hemicolectomy for a proximal transverse colon cancer.

The surgeon uses a robotic setup with hockey stick ports positioned along the left side. The medial to lateral dissection begins beneath the ileocolic pedicle, revealing the ileocolic vessels and exposing the superior mesenteric artery anterior to the vein. The duodenum is identified and cleared to prevent injury during high ligation of the ileocolic vessels. The junction of the ileocolic vein and superior mesenteric vein is divided, followed by dissection and division of the ileocolic artery. The surgeon isolates the right colic vessels while marking the planned line of transection along the anterior surface of the superior mesenteric vessels.

The surgeon isolates the right colic vein and artery, lifting the mesentery off the head of the pancreas. Branches of the loop of Henle are visible. The surgeon marks the line of transection, aiming to remove the tattoo located six centimeters distal to the tumor. The right branch of the middle colic vessels is isolated by thinning the overlying fatty tissue to create a window around it.

The surgeon continues thinning out the tissue using a vessel sealer, removing an enlarged lymph node along the base of the middle colic vessels. The right branch of the middle colic artery and vein are isolated and taken. The surgeon proceeds to dissect the transverse colon mesentery, separating mesogastric fat from the undersurface and sweeping it down, while approaching the colon wall.

The surgeon tucks a sponge underneath to protect the duodenum, then enters the lesser sac to mobilize the hepatic flexure, joining the previously dissected medial space. The omentum fused above the liver is resected, and the pylorus is visible as the tissue is taken up against it.

The surgeon adjusts the patient's position from reverse Trendelenburg to a more supine orientation to improve visualization of the high hepatic flexure. The hepatocolic fat is visible as the surgeon navigates around the flexure beneath the liver. The appendix is lifted off the retroperitoneum, and the dissection proceeds along the white line to connect with the superior dissection.
The surgeon completes the dissection and proceeds to take the small bowel mesentery, marking the end of the procedure.

The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) to check perfusion, with a stapler inserted through the suprapubic port, which will be upsized for extraction. The small bowel is divided, and an isoparastaltic anastomosis is lined up with two stay sutures placed proximally and distally. The distal suture stabilizes the bowel, while the proximal aids in stapler insertion. The surgeon holds the bowel inferiorly to facilitate stapler placement, then cuts the suture to close the common channel using a 3-0, 6-inch relock suture with an inner baseball stitch from the near to the far corner, and then back down.
The operation concludes with the extraction of the specimen through the suprapubic port site.
